edges.description: Mutant chaperone, ER, synthetase, and dynactin proteins disturb protein quality control and axonal transport within the motor neuron.
edges.source: Motor Neuron and Distal Axon Gene Defect

edges.description: Impaired proteostasis and axonal transport preferentially compromise the longest peripheral motor axons, producing a length-dependent distal motor axonopathy.
Chronic motor denervation of distal muscles produces progressive distal weakness and atrophy, beginning in the lower (or, for some subtypes, upper) limbs.

nodes.description: Mutant small heat-shock proteins (HSPB1, HSPB8) and seipin (BSCL2) form intracellular aggregates and impair protein quality control, while mutant dynactin (DCTN1) and tRNA-synthetase (GARS1) defects compromise axonal transport and local translation — collectively undermining maintenance of the long motor axon.

evidence.reference_title: Heterozygous missense mutations in BSCL2 are associated with distal hereditary motor neuropathy and Silver syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The amino acid substitutions N88S and S90L affect glycosylation of seipin and result in aggregate formation leading to neurodegeneration.

evidence.reference_title: Mutant dynactin in motor neuron disease.
evidence.snippet: Binding assays show decreased binding of the mutant protein to microtubules. Our results show that dysfunction of dynactin-mediated transport can lead to human motor neuron disease.

nodes.description: Clinically evident distal limb weakness and muscle atrophy, typically beginning in the feet and lower legs with foot drop and pes cavus, without prominent sensory loss; some subtypes (BSCL2/Silver syndrome, GARS1/CMT2D) have upper-limb predominant hand amyotrophy, and TRPV4-related forms have a scapuloperoneal distribution.

evidence.reference_title: Hot-spot residue in small heat-shock protein 22 causes distal motor neuropathy.
evidence.snippet: pure motor disorders of the peripheral nervous system resulting in severe atrophy and wasting of distal limb muscles

nodes.description: Progressive weakness of distal limb muscles, typically beginning in the lower limbs.

evidence.reference_title: Early onset hereditary neuronopathies: an update on non-5q motor neuron diseases.
evidence.snippet: Hereditary motor neuropathies (HMN) were first defined as a group of neuromuscular disorders characterized by lower motor neuron dysfunction, slowly progressive length-dependent distal muscle weakness and atrophy, without sensory involvement.

nodes.description: Progressive degeneration of the distal portions of the longest motor axons with chronic motor denervation, manifest as neurogenic changes on EMG and a motor axonal pattern on nerve conduction studies with preserved sensory responses.

evidence.reference_title: Hot-spot residue in small heat-shock protein 22 causes distal motor neuropathy.
evidence.snippet: Distal hereditary motor neuropathies are pure motor disorders of the peripheral nervous system resulting in severe atrophy and wasting of distal limb muscles.

nodes.description: Heterozygous (dominant) mutations in motor-neuron and distal-axon genes — small heat-shock protein chaperones (HSPB1, HSPB8), the ER protein seipin (BSCL2), glycyl-tRNA synthetase (GARS1), the dynactin axonal-transport adaptor (DCTN1), the choline transporter (SLC5A7), and the TRPV4 cation channel — impair the homeostatic functions that lower motor neurons require to build and maintain their long peripheral axons. Recurrent molecular consequences are protein misfolding/aggregation, impaired axonal transport, and defective tRNA charging.
nodes.id: Motor Neuron and Distal Axon Gene Defect

evidence.reference_title: Molecular genetics and mechanisms of disease in distal hereditary motor neuropathies: insights directing future genetic studies.
evidence.snippet: The mutated genes identified to-date in dHMN include HSPB1, HSPB8, HSPB3, DCTN1, GARS, PLEKHG5, BSCL2, SETX, IGHMBP2, ATP7A and

nodes.description: Supportive rehabilitation including physical therapy and ankle-foot orthoses to manage distal weakness, foot drop, and gait disturbance.
